title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Show code
library(here)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
Show code
# ── Version identifiers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DataVersion     <- "1_2"
ModelVersion    <- "1_14"
RunVersion      <- "1_14"
FunctionVersion <- "1_4"

SaveName <- paste0("MPVA_Fit_D", DataVersion, "_M", ModelVersion, "_R", RunVersion)

# ── Helper functions ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
source(here("Code", paste0("MPVA_Functions", FunctionVersion, ".R")))

# ── Load data (population names, years, covariates) ─────────────────────────
source(here("Code", paste0("PrepareData_MPVA", DataVersion, ".R")))


Populations: 61 
Core areas:  11 
Years:       39 ( 1984 - 2022 )
Redd sites:  83 
CPUE obs:    0 across 0 sections
temp_mean:   10.054 | temp_sd: 1.535 
All dimension checks passed.


Show code
# ── Load saved model fit ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fit <- readRDS(here("Results", paste0(SaveName, ".RDS")))
list2env(fit, envir = .GlobalEnv)


<environment: R_GlobalEnv>


Show code
pop_core_vec <- CoreArea_Num[match(seq_len(data_list$nPop), LocalPops_Num)]

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Overview
This document presents results from a hierarchical state-space Ricker model for bull trout (Salvelinus confluentus) fitted to redd count time series across multiple local populations nested within core areas.

Spatial Hierarchy
The model uses a three-level spatial structure:



Level
Unit
N




Core area
Watershed complex
11


Local population
Spawning tributary
61


Survey site
Redd count reach
83





Data Coverage


Show code
year_range <- range(DatYears)
n_obs      <- sum(!is.na(data_list$ReddCounts))
n_possible <- prod(dim(data_list$ReddCounts))

data.frame(
  Metric = c("Years", "Core areas", "Local populations",
             "Survey sites", "Site-year observations", "Data coverage"),
  Value  = c(
    paste(year_range[1], "–", year_range[2]),
    data_list$nCore,
    data_list$nPop,
    data_list$nSites,
    n_obs,
    paste0(round(100 * n_obs / n_possible, 1), "%")
  )
) |>
  kable(col.names = c("Metric", "Value")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)





Metric
Value




Years
1984 – 2022


Core areas
11


Local populations
61


Survey sites
83


Site-year observations
1950


Data coverage
60.2%

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Model Description

Process Model
The latent population abundance follows a density-dependent Ricker process:
\[\log N_{p,t} = \log N_{p,t-1} + R_{p,t} + \varepsilon_{p,t}\]
where the instantaneous growth rate is:
\[R_{p,t} = b_{0r,p} + \beta_{\text{Flow},p} \cdot \text{Flow}_{p,t-1}
           + \beta_{\text{Temp},p} \cdot T_{p,t-1}
           + \beta_{\text{Temp}_2} \cdot T_{p,t-1}^2
           + \beta_{\text{INV},p} \cdot \text{INV}_{p,t-1}
           + \beta_{\text{LKT},c} \cdot \text{LKT}_{c,t-1}
           - \phi_p \cdot D_{p,t-1}\]
All dynamic covariates carry an effective lag of 6 years (5-year R-side lag + 1-year C++ indexing), reflecting the time from in-gravel incubation to adult redd counts.


Observation Model
Redd counts at each survey site follow a Poisson distribution:
\[C_{s,y} \sim \text{Poisson}\!\left(0.5 \cdot N_{p,y} \cdot \pi_s\right)\]
where \(\pi_s\) is the fraction of population habitat surveyed at site \(s\). The factor of 0.5 converts spawning adults to females (one redd per female).


Hierarchical Parameter Structure







Parameter
Hierarchy




Baseline growth rate \(b_{0r}\)
Global → Core → Population


Density dependence \(\phi\)
Global → Core → Population


Flow slope \(\beta_\text{Flow}\)
Global hyper-mean → Population


Temperature slope \(\beta_\text{Temp}\)
Global hyper-mean → Population


Invasive slope \(\beta_\text{INV}\)
Global hyper-mean → Population


Lake trout slope \(\beta_\text{LKT}\)
Global hyper-mean → Core area


Adfluvial access, valley bottom
Global scalar on \(b_{0r}\) mean


W95 winter flood frequency, road density
Global scalar on \(b_{0r}\) mean

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Model Fit


Show code
conv_label <- ifelse(optB$convergence == 0, "Converged (0)",
                     paste("Not converged (", optB$convergence, ")"))
max_grad   <- max(abs(fit_rep$gradient.fixed))

data.frame(
  Metric = c("Convergence code", "Negative log-likelihood", "Max |gradient|"),
  Value  = c(conv_label, round(optB$objective, 2), formatC(max_grad, format = "e", digits = 2))
) |>
  kable(col.names = c("Metric", "Value")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)





Metric
Value




Convergence code
Not converged ( 1 )


Negative log-likelihood
8862.06


Max |gradient|
5.87e+00

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Latent Abundance Trajectories
Estimated latent abundances (number of adult spawners) for each local population, smoothed by the state-space model.


Show code
fit_plots <- plot_fit(data_list, fit_rep)






































Worst-fitting sites:
# A tibble: 15 × 6
   pop   site      n  rmse mean_pearson sd_pearson
   <fct> <fct> <int> <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl>
 1 55    75       38  24.8        2.15       2.24 
 2 55    74       38  24.3       -2.03       1.95 
 3 23    31       20  15.1       -2.92       1.20 
 4 28    41       18  14.5       -2.23       1.39 
 5 7     9        33  13.7        0.843      2.24 
 6 7     8        33  13.0       -0.888      1.94 
 7 48    67       27  12.4       -1.28       1.42 
 8 44    62       11  12.3        2.25       2.96 
 9 23    32       34  12.0        1.28       1.56 
10 48    66       29  11.9        2.55       2.96 
11 11    14       23  11.3        3.50       1.61 
12 44    61       11  11.3       -1.71       1.66 
13 61    82       20  11.1       -1.23       1.68 
14 11    13       23  10.7       -2.56       0.805
15 22    30       14  10.6        1.25       2.66 


Show code
dfN       <- fit_plots$latent
df_obsfit <- fit_plots$fit
df_resid  <- fit_plots$resid

dfN |>
  mutate(
    year_actual = DatYears[year],
    pop_name    = U_LocalPops_In[pop],
    core_name   = U_CoreAreas_In[pop_core_vec[pop]]
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year_actual, y = N, color = core_name)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~pop_name, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Core area") +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Latent Abundance (N)",
       title = "Estimated Bull Trout Spawner Abundance by Population") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        strip.text = element_text(size = 7))

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Observed vs Fitted Redd Counts


Show code
df_obsfit |>
  mutate(
    year_actual = DatYears[year],
    pop_name    = U_LocalPops_In[pop]
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year_actual)) +
  geom_point(aes(y = y_obs), alpha = 0.6, size = 1.2, color = "grey40") +
  geom_line(aes(y = mu), linewidth = 0.8, color = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~pop_name, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Redd Counts",
       title = "Observed (points) vs Fitted (line) Redd Counts",
       caption = "Blue line = Poisson mean (0.5 × N × survey fraction); grey points = observed counts") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 7))











Show code
df_obsfit |>
  ggplot(aes(x = mu, y = y_obs)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1.5) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "steelblue", linetype = 2, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log1p", labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(trans = "log1p", labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(x = "Fitted (Poisson mean, log1p scale)", y = "Observed (log1p scale)",
       title = "Observed vs Fitted — All Site-Years") +
  theme_bw()

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Residual Diagnostics


Show code
df_resid |>
  mutate(
    year_actual = DatYears[year],
    pop_name    = U_LocalPops_In[pop]
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year_actual, y = pearson)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~pop_name, ncol = 4) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Pearson Residual",
       title = "Pearson Residuals Through Time") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 7))











Show code
df_resid |>
  ggplot(aes(x = pearson)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey40") +
  labs(x = "Pearson Residual", y = "Count",
       title = "Residual Distribution — All Site-Years") +
  theme_bw()

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Covariate Effects

Global Scalar Effects on Baseline Growth Rate
These covariates shift the prior mean of the population-level growth rate intercept (\(b_{0r,p}\)).


Show code
extract_fe <- function(par_name) {
  v  <- fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == par_name]
  se <- fit_rep$sd[names(fit_rep$value)    == par_name]
  if (!length(v)) return(NULL)
  data.frame(Parameter = par_name, Estimate = v[1], SE = se[1],
             Lower95 = v[1] - 1.96 * se[1],
             Upper95 = v[1] + 1.96 * se[1])
}

global_pars <- c("bAdf", "bVB", "bFlood", "bRD", "bLKT_M")
global_df   <- bind_rows(lapply(global_pars, extract_fe)) |>
  mutate(
    Label = c("Adfluvial access", "Valley bottom width (VB)",
              "W95 winter flood frequency", "Road density (valley bottom)",
              "Lake trout ratio (cross-system mean)"),
    sig = ifelse(Lower95 > 0 | Upper95 < 0, "*", "")
  )

global_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Label, Estimate), y = Estimate)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Lower95, ymax = Upper95), width = 0.25, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sig, y = Upper95 + 0.02), size = 5, color = "tomato") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Coefficient estimate (95% CI)",
       title = "Static Covariate Effects on Baseline Growth Rate",
       caption = "* = 95% CI excludes zero") +
  theme_bw()











Thermal Performance Curve
Bull trout growth rate shows a unimodal response to water temperature (linear + negative quadratic).


Show code
if (covar_config$use_Temp == 1) {
  plot_temp_curve(fit_rep, temp_mean = temp_mean, temp_sd = temp_sd)
}


Thermal optimum (z-scored): -1.127 
Thermal optimum (raw scale): 8.32 











Population-Level Flow Slopes
Each local population has its own sensitivity to summer streamflow, drawn from a global hyper-distribution (dashed red line = hyper-mean).


Show code
if (covar_config$use_Flow == 1) {
  bFlow_vals <- fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "bFlow_pop"]
  bFlow_ses  <- fit_rep$sd[names(fit_rep$value)    == "bFlow_pop"]
  bFlow_M    <- fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "bFlow_M"][1]

  data.frame(
    pop_name  = U_LocalPops_In,
    core_name = U_CoreAreas_In[pop_core_vec],
    estimate  = bFlow_vals,
    se        = bFlow_ses
  ) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = reorder(pop_name, estimate), y = estimate, color = core_name)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0,       linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = bFlow_M, linetype = 3, color = "tomato", linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * se,
                      ymax = estimate + 1.96 * se), width = 0.4, alpha = 0.7) +
    geom_point(size = 2.5) +
    annotate("text", x = 1, y = bFlow_M,
             label = paste0("Hyper-mean = ", round(bFlow_M, 3)),
             hjust = 0, vjust = -0.6, color = "tomato", size = 3.5) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Core area") +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Flow slope (bFlow_pop)",
         title = "Summer Streamflow Effect on Growth Rate by Population") +
    theme_bw() +
    theme(legend.position = "right")
}











Population-Level Temperature Slopes


Show code
if (covar_config$use_Temp == 1) {
  bTemp_vals <- fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "bTemp_pop"]
  bTemp_ses  <- fit_rep$sd[names(fit_rep$value)    == "bTemp_pop"]
  bTemp_M    <- fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "bTemp_M"][1]

  data.frame(
    pop_name  = U_LocalPops_In,
    core_name = U_CoreAreas_In[pop_core_vec],
    estimate  = bTemp_vals,
    se        = bTemp_ses
  ) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = reorder(pop_name, estimate), y = estimate, color = core_name)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0,       linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = bTemp_M, linetype = 3, color = "tomato", linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * se,
                      ymax = estimate + 1.96 * se), width = 0.4, alpha = 0.7) +
    geom_point(size = 2.5) +
    annotate("text", x = 1, y = bTemp_M,
             label = paste0("Hyper-mean = ", round(bTemp_M, 3)),
             hjust = 0, vjust = -0.6, color = "tomato", size = 3.5) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Core area") +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Temperature linear slope (bTemp_pop)",
         title = "August Temperature Linear Effect on Growth Rate by Population",
         caption = "Note: global quadratic (bTemp2) is negative — combined effect is unimodal") +
    theme_bw() +
    theme(legend.position = "right")
}











Population-Level Invasive Species Slopes


Show code
if (covar_config$use_INV == 1) {
  bINV_vals <- fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "bINV_pop"]
  bINV_ses  <- fit_rep$sd[names(fit_rep$value)    == "bINV_pop"]
  bINV_M    <- fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "bINV_M"][1]

  data.frame(
    pop_name  = U_LocalPops_In,
    core_name = U_CoreAreas_In[pop_core_vec],
    estimate  = bINV_vals,
    se        = bINV_ses
  ) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = reorder(pop_name, estimate), y = estimate, color = core_name)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0,      linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = bINV_M, linetype = 3, color = "tomato", linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * se,
                      ymax = estimate + 1.96 * se), width = 0.4, alpha = 0.7) +
    geom_point(size = 2.5) +
    annotate("text", x = 1, y = bINV_M,
             label = paste0("Hyper-mean = ", round(bINV_M, 3)),
             hjust = 0, vjust = -0.6, color = "tomato", size = 3.5) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Core area") +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Invasive ratio slope (bINV_pop)",
         title = "Non-Native Trout Ratio Effect on Growth Rate by Population") +
    theme_bw() +
    theme(legend.position = "right")
}











Core-Level Lake Trout Slopes


Show code
if (covar_config$use_LKT == 1) {
  bLKT_vals <- fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "bLKT_core"]
  bLKT_ses  <- fit_rep$sd[names(fit_rep$value)    == "bLKT_core"]
  bLKT_M    <- fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "bLKT_M"][1]

  data.frame(
    core_name = U_CoreAreas_In,
    estimate  = bLKT_vals,
    se        = bLKT_ses
  ) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = reorder(core_name, estimate), y = estimate)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0,      linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = bLKT_M, linetype = 3, color = "tomato", linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = estimate - 1.96 * se,
                      ymax = estimate + 1.96 * se), width = 0.25, linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +
    annotate("text", x = 1, y = bLKT_M,
             label = paste0("Hyper-mean = ", round(bLKT_M, 3)),
             hjust = 0, vjust = -0.6, color = "tomato", size = 3.5) +
    coord_flip() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Lake trout slope (bLKT_core)",
         title = "Lake Trout Ratio Effect on Growth Rate by Core Area") +
    theme_bw()
}

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Growth Rate and Density Dependence

Baseline Growth Rates by Population


Show code
b0r_df <- data.frame(
  pop_name  = U_LocalPops_In,
  core_name = U_CoreAreas_In[pop_core_vec],
  b0r       = fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "b0r_pop"],
  b0r_se    = fit_rep$sd[names(fit_rep$value)    == "b0r_pop"]
)

ggplot(b0r_df, aes(x = reorder(pop_name, b0r), y = b0r, color = core_name)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = b0r - 1.96 * b0r_se,
                    ymax = b0r + 1.96 * b0r_se), width = 0.4, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Core area") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Baseline growth rate (b0r_pop)",
       title = "Population-Level Baseline Growth Rates",
       caption = "Positive = growing at mean covariate conditions; negative = declining") +
  theme_bw()











Variance Components


Show code
sigma_pars <- c(
  "sigmaN"          = "Process error (σ_N)",
  "sigma_b0r_core"  = "Growth rate: core SD",
  "sigma_b0r_pop"   = "Growth rate: pop SD",
  "sigma_b0phi_pop" = "Density dependence: pop SD",
  "sigma_bFlow"     = "Flow slope: pop SD",
  "sigma_bTemp"     = "Temp slope: pop SD",
  "sigma_bINV"      = "Invasive slope: pop SD",
  "sigma_bLKT"      = "Lake trout slope: core SD"
)

sigma_df <- lapply(names(sigma_pars), function(nm) {
  v  <- fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == nm]
  se <- fit_rep$sd[names(fit_rep$value)    == nm]
  if (!length(v)) return(NULL)
  data.frame(Label = sigma_pars[nm], Estimate = v[1], SE = se[1])
}) |> bind_rows()

sigma_df |>
  mutate(Lower95 = pmax(0, Estimate - 1.96 * SE),
         Upper95 = Estimate + 1.96 * SE) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Label, Estimate), y = Estimate)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Lower95, ymax = Upper95), width = 0.25, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Standard deviation estimate (95% CI)",
       title = "Variance Component Estimates") +
  theme_bw()

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Population Table
Summary of estimated baseline growth rates and density dependence for each local population.


Show code
phi_pop <- exp(fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "log_b0phi_pop"]) / 1000

data.frame(
  Population  = U_LocalPops_In,
  `Core Area` = U_CoreAreas_In[pop_core_vec],
  `b0r`       = round(b0r_df$b0r, 3),
  `b0r SE`    = round(b0r_df$b0r_se, 3),
  `phi (fish/km)` = round(phi_pop, 4)
) |>
  kable(col.names = c("Population", "Core Area", "b0r", "SE", "φ (fish/km)"),
        align     = c("l", "l", "r", "r", "r")) |>

title: R Notebook
text:
This is an R Markdown Notebook. When you execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code.
Try executing this chunk by clicking the Run button within the chunk or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing Cmd+Shift+Enter.


Show code
plot(cars)





Add a new chunk by clicking the Insert Chunk button on the toolbar or by pressing Cmd+Option+I.
When you save the notebook, an HTML file containing the code and output will be saved alongside it (click the Preview button or press Cmd+Shift+K to preview the HTML file).
The preview shows you a rendered HTML copy of the contents of the editor. Consequently, unlike Knit, Preview does not run any R code chunks. Instead, the output of the chunk when it was last run in the editor is displayed.

title: Bull Trout Species Status Process
text: Bull trout (Salvelinus confluentus) are a cold-water obligate salmonid native to western North America and listed as Threatened under the U.S. Endangered Species Act. This site presents a hierarchical state-space population model developed as part of the Bull Trout Species Status Process (SSP) to synthesize redd count monitoring data across the Northern Rocky Mountains and assess population status and vulnerability.

title: Bull Trout Species Status Process
text:
What this model does
The model integrates long-term redd count time series (1984–2022) from 61 local populations nested within 11 core areas to estimate:

Population-specific growth rates and how they vary with environmental conditions
Density dependence at the population level
Effects of climate (summer streamflow, August water temperature) on population growth
Biotic threats from non-native trout and lake trout
Habitat quality indicators (adfluvial life history access, valley bottom area, W95 winter flood frequency, road density)

title: Bull Trout Species Status Process
text:
Site structure



Data
Explore the raw redd count time series and environmental covariate data for each core area and local population. Use the right-side table of contents to navigate directly to the populations under your purview.



Model Results
View fitted population trajectories, observed vs. fitted redd counts, covariate effect estimates (including population-specific random slopes), and model variance components.



About the model
The model is a hierarchical state-space Ricker model fitted with TMB (Template Model Builder). Random effects are integrated out via the Laplace approximation. Parameter uncertainty is propagated via the delta method.

title: Bull Trout Species Status Process
text:
Species background
Bull trout require cold, clear, complex streams for spawning and rearing. They are highly sensitive to:







Threat
Mechanism




Warming water temperatures
Upper thermal tolerance ~13–15 °C for spawning adults; lethal above ~20 °C


Altered hydrology
Peak flow timing and winter flood frequency affect gravel incubation success


Non-native trout
Competitive displacement and hybridization (brook trout); predation (lake trout)


Road density
Fine sediment delivery to spawning gravels; barrier culverts


Loss of migratory access
Adfluvial populations (lake-resident adults) have higher productivity




Model version 1.14 · Data version 1.2 · Source code at github.com/timothycline/BullTroutSSP
